section: 6.3.1 Observe and identify appearance of materials
learningObjective: Identify lustrous vs non-lustrous materials by surface appearance and understand that lustre is not exclusive to metals.

steps.title: Sort by Lustre
steps.description: Drag each object into Lustrous or Non-lustrous, or tap a tile then tap a bin. Read the reason on every correct drop.

additionalProps.iconNote: Each object renders a purpose-drawn inline SVG icon selected by 'iconKey' (the 'emoji' field is kept only as a fallback/label aid and is not rendered). When adding a new object, set iconKey to one of availableIconKeys.

objects.name: Freshly Cut Iron
objects.reason: A freshly cut metal surface shines brightly — that is lustre.
objects.hint: Look at the freshly cut surface, not the old rusted side!

objects.name: Polished Silver Bangle
objects.reason: Polished silver has a bright, mirror-like shine — classic lustre.
objects.hint: Polished metals like silver, gold, copper are lustrous.

objects.name: New Steel Spoon
objects.reason: New steel reflects light — shiny means lustrous.
objects.hint: A new steel utensil shines brightly when light falls on it.

objects.name: Old Aluminium Pan
objects.reason: Aluminium keeps its shine fairly well — its surface still reflects light.
objects.hint: Aluminium does not tarnish much. Its surface still shines.

objects.name: Gold Ring
objects.reason: Gold stays bright and shiny for years without tarnishing — a classic lustrous metal.
objects.hint: Gold jewellery shines beautifully — it is a lustrous metal.

objects.name: Brass Door Handle
objects.reason: A polished brass handle reflects light with a warm golden shine — lustrous.
objects.hint: Shiny metal fittings like brass handles are lustrous.

objects.name: Tarnished Copper Vessel
objects.reason: Air and moisture made the copper dull — the surface looks non-lustrous now.
objects.hint: Tarnish hides the shine! Old copper turns dull — non-lustrous now.

objects.name: Raw Wooden Block
objects.reason: Wood has a rough, dull surface — non-lustrous.
objects.hint: Wood, paper, jute — all have dull surfaces.

objects.name: Rubber Eraser
objects.reason: Rubber has a soft, dull, matte surface — non-lustrous.

objects.name: Leaf
objects.reason: A leaf has a soft, dull surface — non-lustrous.
objects.hint: Natural surfaces like leaves are dull, not shiny like metals.

objects.name: Coal Lump
objects.reason: Coal is black, rough and dull — clearly non-lustrous.
objects.hint: Coal is a famous non-lustrous example from your textbook.

objects.name: Brick
objects.reason: A brick has a rough, dry, dull surface — non-lustrous.
objects.hint: Bricks are rough and never reflect light — non-lustrous.

features.scoreStrip: Tracks correct, wrong, timer, and total in the header
features.feedbackOnCorrect: Tile locks in green and one-line reason appears
features.feedbackOnWrong: Tile shakes red and a hint linking to the error pattern appears
features.completion: Trophy banner + stats card (correct/wrong/time) + key takeaway + Try Again
features.interaction: Tap-and-tap or drag-and-drop. Drag uses Pointer Events with pointer capture, so dragging works on mouse, touch, and pen across all devices (phones, tablets, desktops). A floating ghost tile follows the finger/cursor and bins highlight on hover.
features.icons: Each object shows a purpose-drawn inline SVG icon (no emoji glyphs, no external icon libraries) chosen via its iconKey field.
features.shuffled: Tiles shuffle on each session for replay value
features.responsive: Bins stack vertically on mobile, side-by-side from 640px up
features.themeAlignment: Singularity design — Poppins font, purple/orange palette, rounded 14–20px

studentInstructions: Drag each object into Lustrous or Non-lustrous (or tap a tile then tap a bin). Read the reason on every correct drop.
errorAlertPattern: Age alone does not decide shine — check the freshly cut surface. Tarnish ≠ never lustrous. Shiny metals like gold, silver, steel and brass are lustrous, while wood, rubber, coal and brick are dull (non-lustrous).
